title: Monocular Depth Estimation
concept: Monocular depth estimation predicts depth from a single camera image using a trained CNN, learning to infer distance cues like relative object size, perspective lines, and known object scale — no second camera required.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does stereo depth estimation struggle on a large, textureless, plain-colored surface?

options.text: There are few distinctive features to match between the two camera views, making disparity estimation unreliable
Plain surfaces physically block camera sensors
Stereo cameras cannot capture any plain-colored image

exercises.explain: Yes! Feature matching needs distinctive texture, which plain surfaces lack.

cards.title: Most cameras only have one eye
cards.body: Your smartphone (mostly), most security cameras, and countless existing camera systems only have ONE lens — no second camera for stereo triangulation. Can we still estimate depth with just one image?

cards.title: Monocular Depth Estimation
cards.body: Monocular depth estimation predicts a full depth map from a SINGLE 2D image using a trained CNN. Instead of geometric triangulation (which needs two cameras), the network learns to infer depth from learned visual cues like object size, perspective lines, texture gradients, and known typical object scales.

cards.title: How painters fake depth with one eye's worth of canvas
cards.body: A classical painter creates a convincing sense of depth on a flat, single canvas using techniques like making distant objects smaller and hazier, and converging perspective lines toward a vanishing point. A monocular depth CNN learns very similar visual cues from data, without ever needing a second 'eye'.

cards.title: AR furniture placement apps
cards.body: A phone app lets you visualize how a virtual sofa would look in your living room using just the single rear camera. It uses monocular depth estimation to understand the room's 3D layout well enough to place the virtual furniture at a realistic scale and position.

cards.title: It's a learned guess, not true geometric measurement
cards.body: It's a common misconception that monocular depth is as precise as stereo or laser-based (LiDAR) measurement. Since there's no true geometric triangulation with a single camera, monocular depth is fundamentally a learned STATISTICAL estimate — usually good enough for many applications, but less precise and more prone to systematic errors than true multi-sensor depth.

cards.title: Stereo vs. Monocular Depth
cards.body: The core tradeoff between the two approaches:

left.text:
Needs two cameras, fixed apart
True geometric triangulation
More precise, more hardware

right.text:
Needs only ONE camera
Learned statistical estimate from cues
Less precise, works on any single-camera setup

exercises.prompt: How does monocular depth estimation infer distance without a second camera?

options.text: By learning visual cues like object size, perspective lines, and typical object scale from training data
By physically triangulating between two lenses
By measuring the exact time light takes to bounce back, like radar
It is mathematically impossible without a second camera

exercises.explain: Correct! Monocular depth relies on learned visual cues rather than geometric triangulation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Monocular depth estimation is fundamentally as precise as true geometric stereo triangulation.
exercises.explain: False. Without a second camera for true triangulation, monocular depth is a learned statistical estimate, generally less precise.

exercises.sentence: Monocular depth estimation predicts distance from a ___ image using a trained CNN.
exercises.explain: Only one camera image is needed, unlike stereo methods requiring two.

exercises.prompt: Why might an AR furniture app prefer monocular depth estimation over requiring a specialized stereo camera rig?

options.text: Most smartphones only have compatible single-camera setups, so monocular methods work on far more existing devices
Monocular depth is always more accurate than stereo depth
Stereo cameras cannot be used for any AR application

exercises.explain: Correct! Broad device compatibility is a major practical advantage of monocular methods.

exercises.prompt: A robotics team needs highly precise depth measurements (within millimeters) for delicate assembly-line manipulation. Should they rely solely on monocular depth estimation?

options.text: Probably not alone — monocular depth is a learned statistical estimate, less precise than true geometric methods like stereo or LiDAR needed for millimeter-level precision
Yes, monocular depth is always exactly as precise as laser-based measurement
Depth estimation of any kind is completely unnecessary for assembly tasks
Monocular depth cannot be used in any robotics application whatsoever

exercises.explain: Exactly! High-precision tasks typically need more precise sensing than monocular estimation alone provides. Next: the datasets and evaluation challenges specific to depth estimation.
